name: Big Five Profiling of Fictional Characters
core_claim: The Big Five (Five-Factor Model / OCEAN: Openness to experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism) is the dominant empirically-derived model of human personality, claiming that the variance in trait descriptions across persons reduces to five broadly orthogonal continuous dimensions. The 'Big Five profiling of fictional characters' line of work (e.g. Ramirez-Sanchez et al., 'Exploring Personality Traits: A Big Five Profiling of Fictional Movie Characters', Computacion y Sistemas, 2024; the Story2Personality benchmark, Sang et al., Findings of EMNLP 2022; Harry Potter character profiling studies; the historical-persons profiling of Jacobs et al., arXiv:2106.07237) makes a transferred claim: that fictional characters, although authored constructs, carry coherent personality signatures that can be located on the same five dimensions, and that these signatures can be RECOVERED computationally from text the character produces (dialogue) and text describing the character (scene/stage descriptions, narration). The central claim for narrative engagement is therefore NOT about plot, topic, or felt emotion but about WHO the character is as a stable dispositional type — a high-Openness, low-Conscientiousness rogue; a high-Neuroticism, high-Conscientiousness striver — on the bet that 'character feel' is a real, namable, matchable attribute and that viewers gravitate to recurring character types. As applied to HelpME2C, the proposition is to make character-personality a taxonomy axis so two viewers who both gravitate to, say, warm-but-chaotic protagonists (high Agreeableness, low Conscientiousness) can be matched on that shared appetite.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Big Five character profiling occupies a distinct level from both 'descriptive/topical' (what the story is about) and 'experiential/affective' (what it feels like to watch). It is best classed as DESCRIPTIVE-but-CHARACTEROLOGICAL: it describes a stable property of an entity WITHIN the work (the protagonist's disposition), not a property of the work as a whole and not the viewer's felt response. This is an important third axis. A topical tag ('heist-and-caper') says what happens; an affective tag ('evokes-tension') says how it lands; a Big Five character profile says what kind of person you are spending two hours with. It is adjacent to but distinct from 'character-study' in the existing texture vocabulary: 'character-study' flags that the work foregrounds interiority as its mode, whereas Big Five profiling specifies the CONTENT of that interiority (which traits, high or low). The framework is structural in that the five axes are fixed and continuous, but its object is a person-construct, not narrative form. Crucially it is receiver-independent at the level of the trait estimate (the character's Extraversion is a property inferable from the text) but becomes relational the moment you ask whether a viewer ENJOYS that trait profile — that appetite is the matchable signal, and it lives on the viewer side.
key_dimensions: Five continuous bipolar dimensions, each conventionally decomposed into six facets (NEO-PI-R facet structure): (1) OPENNESS to experience — imagination, artistic interests, emotionality, adventurousness, intellect/curiosity, liberalism (vs conventional, concrete, routine-preferring). (2) CONSCIENTIOUSNESS — self-efficacy, orderliness, dutifulness, achievement-striving, self-discipline, cautiousness (vs careless, spontaneous, disorganised). (3) EXTRAVERSION — friendliness, gregariousness, assertiveness, activity level, excitement-seeking, cheerfulness (vs reserved, solitary, low-key). (4) AGREEABLENESS — trust, morality/straightforwardness, altruism, cooperation, modesty, sympathy (vs antagonistic, competitive, skeptical, callous). (5) NEUROTICISM / (inverse) Emotional Stability — anxiety, anger/hostility, depression, self-consciousness, immoderation, vulnerability (vs calm, resilient, even-tempered). For character profiling the candidate-vocabulary axes are the five trait poles, optionally graded (low / mid / high) and optionally facet-level (e.g. high-anger-hostility vs high-anxiety within Neuroticism). Some profiling studies also report a per-character emotion profile alongside the trait profile (e.g. the historical-persons work pairs Big Five with emotion lexica), but the five traits are the spine.
cross_cultural_validity: The Big Five itself has the strongest cross-cultural evidence of any personality taxonomy: the five-factor structure has been replicated via translated questionnaires across many languages and cultures (McCrae & Costa's cross-cultural programme; the lexical tradition). However, replication is imperfect — the structure is cleaner in WEIRD/educated samples; in some non-Western, less-literate, or collectivist samples the five factors do not cleanly emerge (the Tsimane study in Bolivia famously failed to recover the five-factor structure), and alternative dimensional counts (six-factor HEXACO adding Honesty-Humility; two-factor 'Big Two') are competitive. For the FICTIONAL-CHARACTER application the cross-cultural picture is weaker still and largely untested: the profiling studies to date are overwhelmingly on English-language Western media (Hollywood movie scripts, Harry Potter, Shakespeare) and the trait LABELS are applied in English. Whether an LLM profiling a Korean, Japanese, or Nigerian character recovers a culturally faithful trait profile — versus projecting an Anglocentric reading of, say, modesty or assertiveness, which are culturally valenced — is essentially unstudied. Expression norms differ: low surface Extraversion in a culture that prizes restraint is not the same construct as low Extraversion in one that prizes self-promotion, and an LLM scoring dialogue against English-trained norms will likely mis-rate. Treat cross-cultural validity of the CHARACTER application as low-confidence even though the underlying model is comparatively robust. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Two layers must be separated. (1) The Big Five as a model of HUMAN personality is among the most empirically grounded constructs in psychology — factor-analytic derivation, behavioural-genetic heritability estimates, longitudinal stability, and predictive validity for life outcomes (job performance, health, relationship outcomes). (2) The CHARACTER-PROFILING application is far less grounded and, critically, has NOT been validated against narrative enjoyment, completion, or preference data — no study shown here demonstrates that matching viewers on preferred character trait-profiles predicts joint satisfaction or co-watching. The evidence that exists is COMPUTATIONAL and benchmark-oriented: Story2Personality (EMNLP-Findings 2022) built ground-truth character labels from a fan/community source (Personality Database-style MBTI and Big Five votes) and showed the prediction task is HARD — no model substantially beat random guessing, inputs are very long (>10K words/character), and joint modelling of verbal (dialogue) and non-verbal (scene description) views helped only modestly. Other studies use external human raters scoring characters on trait scales, or prompt LLMs to 'answer the NEO questionnaire as character X' and treat the output as the profile — convenient but circular (it measures the LLM's stereotype of the character, not a validated trait). So evidence quality is: model itself = strong/experimental; character extraction = weak/computational with poor benchmark performance; link to enjoyment = ABSENT. To validate LLM-extracted character profiles for HelpME2C one would need: (a) human-rater agreement on character trait profiles as gold standard; (b) LLM-vs-human agreement on those profiles from synopsis-scale text; and (c) the missing causal test — whether shared preferred-character-profile predicts joint watch satisfaction.
operationalisability: This is the framework's biggest weakness for HelpME2C specifically, because our extraction unit is the SYNOPSIS, and the profiling literature is unanimous that reliable Big Five character estimation needs FULL DIALOGUE plus scene descriptions (>10K words), not a 1-3 sentence synopsis. The natural extraction unit is the CHARACTER across the whole work (per-character, not per-work), which is itself a mismatch: a synopsis names a protagonist and maybe gives one or two adjectives, which is far too thin to score five continuous dimensions. Failure modes are severe: (1) DATA STARVATION — synopses rarely contain enough behavioural evidence; the LLM will fill gaps with genre/archetype priors ('detective' -> high Conscientiousness, low Agreeableness) producing stereotyped, non-discriminating profiles. (2) WHICH CHARACTER — multi-protagonist or ensemble works have no single profile; the unit is ambiguous. (3) ARC vs STATE — characters change (redemption arcs, breakdowns); a single static five-number profile flattens the very development that defines many dramas. (4) NARRATOR/AUTHOR FRAMING — a character described sympathetically vs unreliably shifts the apparent profile. (5) LOW BENCHMARK CEILING — even with full scripts, SOTA barely beats random, so synopsis-level extraction confidence should be treated as low. (6) FACET COLLAPSE — synopsis can at best support a coarse high/low on one or two SALIENT traits (an obviously anxious, an obviously charming character), not a full five-dimensional facet-level vector. Verdict: full Big Five profiling is NOT reliably synopsis-extractable; only coarse, salient single-trait flags ('anxious-protagonist', 'charismatic-rogue') are defensible, and these need richer data sources (full dialogue, character wikis, fan databases) to be trustworthy.
motivation_served: Maps primarily to IDENTITY EXPRESSION and PARASOCIAL/CHARACTER ATTACHMENT — the appeal of spending time with a particular kind of person, identifying with them, or enjoying a type one finds magnetic (the lovable rogue, the competent stoic, the chaotic genius). It has a hedonic component (some viewers seek the pleasure of charismatic, high-Extraversion leads) and a eudaimonic one (the appreciation of a flawed, high-Neuroticism character study as meaningful). For GROUP viewing it offers a concrete and underused negotiation handle distinct from genre and emotion: two people may share a taste for morally-grey, low-Agreeableness antiheroes even while disagreeing on genre, or one may be repelled by exactly the high-Neuroticism protagonist the other loves. The matchable object is each viewer's PREFERRED character-trait profile (an appetite), and the recommender can find works whose central character fits the overlap, or flag a clash ('one of you avoids unlikeable protagonists'). This is a different and complementary lever to 'what is it about' and 'how does it feel'.
receiver_dependence: Cleanly SPLIT, and the split is the whole design point. The character's trait profile itself is a WORK-SIDE / quasi-absolute property: the protagonist's Extraversion is, in principle, estimable from the text independent of who is watching (subject to the extraction reliability caveats above). But whether that profile is a POSITIVE or NEGATIVE signal is entirely RECEIVER-DEPENDENT — one viewer is drawn to high-Neuroticism, low-Conscientiousness messy protagonists; another finds them exhausting; a third demands competent, high-Conscientiousness leads. There is also a known similarity-vs-complementarity question (do people prefer characters like themselves, or aspirational opposites?) that is itself receiver-dependent and unresolved. For the two-person recommender the correct architecture mirrors the Plutchik treatment: store a WORK-SIDE central-character trait profile (or coarse salient-trait flags) as an attribute of the title, and model each viewer's CHARACTER-TYPE PREFERENCE separately as a user-side profile learned from their history. The joint problem matches one work-side character signature against two user-side appetite profiles, surfacing both overlap and veto ('avoids unlikeable leads').
blind_spots: (1) It models a STATIC TRAIT VECTOR and is blind to CHARACTER ARC — the transformation that is the point of most drama (a coward becoming brave is invisible to a single five-number profile). (2) It says nothing about PLOT, THEME, MORALITY-AS-STAKES, SETTING, or FELT EMOTION; two films with identically-profiled protagonists can be utterly different experiences. (3) It privileges a SINGLE PROTAGONIST and handles ensembles, dual leads, and antagonist-driven stories poorly. (4) It captures DISPOSITION but not RELATIONSHIPS — the dynamic between two characters (banter, rivalry, romance chemistry) is often the actual draw and is not a per-character trait. (5) Big Five omits constructs highly relevant to character appeal: HONESTY-HUMILITY (HEXACO), MORAL ALIGNMENT, competence/intelligence, humour, sexiness/charisma-as-distinct-from-Extraversion — viewers talk in these terms, the five factors do not. (6) It cannot represent INTENTIONALLY FLAT or archetypal characters (genre fiction, fairy tale) without over-reading. (7) The villain/likeability dimension that drives a lot of viewing choice is only obliquely captured (low Agreeableness conflates 'menacing villain' with 'prickly-but-good ally'). (8) No link to enjoyment is established. (9) It is blind to PERFORMANCE — the same written character is a different draw played by different actors, which no script-derived profile captures.
vocabulary_implications: Big Five supplies a CHARACTER-DISPOSITION facet, orthogonal to the existing topical/moral/structural slugs and to the proposed Plutchik evoked-emotion facet. But given the severe synopsis-extractability limits, the recommendation is NOT to encode a full five-dimensional facet-level vector. Instead encode a SMALL controlled set of COARSE, SALIENT central-character flags that an LLM can defensibly tag from synopsis-plus-character-metadata, marked lower-confidence and ideally backstopped with richer data (full dialogue, fan personality databases, character wikis). Proposed work-side slugs (central-character disposition, multi-select, coarse): 'charismatic-extravert-lead', 'reserved-stoic-lead', 'anxious-neurotic-protagonist', 'volatile-hot-tempered-lead', 'meticulous-disciplined-lead', 'chaotic-impulsive-lead', 'warm-empathetic-lead', 'abrasive-antagonistic-lead', 'curious-unconventional-lead', 'conventional-grounded-lead', 'morally-grey-antihero' (cross-cuts Agreeableness x morality). STRONG flags: (a) full Big Five facet vectors are NOT synopsis-extractable — do not attempt; (b) character ARC is not representable in a static flag and may need a separate 'undergoes-transformation' marker; (c) the ENSEMBLE case needs either a per-character store (richer data) or an 'ensemble-no-single-lead' flag; (d) the USER's preferred-character-type is a separate user-side learned profile, NOT a synopsis attribute; (e) all character flags should carry a confidence/provenance tag because synopsis-only extraction performs near random in the literature. Where a fan personality database (Personality Database-style) or character wiki exists, prefer it as the extraction source over the synopsis.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Big Five operates at a NEW LAYER — central-character disposition — that the current HelpME2C vocabulary barely touches, so it mostly ADDS a facet rather than validating existing slugs. It directly validates and refines 'character-study' (texture): character-study marks the MODE, Big Five flags would specify the disposition being studied. It cross-cuts and enriches identity slugs — 'identity-crisis', 'impostor-syndrome', 'mental-illness-portrayal', 'addiction-and-recovery', 'trauma-survival' all co-occur with high-Neuroticism / low-Emotional-Stability central characters; 'finding-purpose' and 'coming-of-age' often pair with rising Conscientiousness or Openness arcs. It enriches relationship slugs ('toxic-relationship', 'forbidden-love') where partner trait profiles drive the dynamic, and moral slugs ('vigilante-justice', 'moral-compromise', 'complicity-with-evil') which frequently centre low-Agreeableness or morally-grey leads — suggesting a useful 'morally-grey-antihero' addition. It reveals a clear GAP: HelpME2C currently encodes what stories are ABOUT and (proposed) how they FEEL, but not WHO you spend time with — the character-disposition axis is genuinely missing. It does NOT suggest any existing slug is misaligned. It is complementary to, not a substitute for, the Plutchik/Reagan affective facets (disposition vs evoked-emotion vs emotional-trajectory) and to genre. A caution it raises: because character profiling is poorly extractable from synopsis, any character-disposition slugs should be lower-confidence than topical slugs and ideally sourced from richer character data.
key_sources: Ramirez-Sanchez et al. (2024), 'Exploring Personality Traits: A Big Five Profiling of Fictional Movie Characters', Computacion y Sistemas, CIC-IPN (FREE PDF: https://polibits.cidetec.ipn.mx/ojs/index.php/CyS/article/viewFile/5294/3821 ; ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/388097904 ) — the namesake study. Sang, Mou, et al. (2022), 'MBTI Personality Prediction for Fictional Characters Using Movie Scripts' / Story2Personality benchmark, Findings of EMNLP 2022 (FREE: ACL Anthology https://aclanthology.org/2022.findings-emnlp.500/ ; arXiv:2210.10994 https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.10994 ) — shows the task is near-random-hard, long inputs, multi-view verbal+non-verbal. Jacobs & Kinder (2021), 'Is Einstein more agreeable and less neurotic than Hitler? A computational exploration of emotional and personality profiles of historical persons', arXiv:2106.07237 (FREE: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.07237 ) — Big Five + emotion profiling from text. 'Revealing Personality Traits: A New Benchmark Dataset for Explainable Personality Recognition on Dialogues' (FREE: https://arxiv.org/html/2409.19723v1 ). 'Personality recognition in Digital Humanities: A review of computational approaches', Digital Scholarship in the Humanities, Oxford (PAYWALLED: https://academic.oup.com/dsh/article/38/4/1646/7207347 ). Foundational model: McCrae & Costa, NEO-PI-R / Five-Factor Model; cross-cultural caveat: Gurven et al. (2013) Tsimane study (the five-factor structure failed to replicate). Big Five — Wikipedia (FREE overview incl. criticisms and HEXACO alternative: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_Five_personality_traits ).
